quotes.verbatim_quote:
Observe the devices used in the arrangements as marked, and it would be very beneficial to actually
learn these arrangements – getting your ‘hands dirty’ with some examples is one of the best ways to
internalize the techniques that are being used, which you can then apply to your own arrangements.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States O'Rourke's core pedagogical instruction for this module: internalize technique through hands-on study of complete arrangements, not abstract reading.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This rendition is a good example of using arpeggiation to fill in the long spaces between each melody
note in this slow ballad.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the load-bearing arranging device for slow ballads in O'Rourke's method: arpeggiation as a melody-space filler.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Diads are also used frequently to provide support to the melody line.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies diads as a primary melody-support device in O'Rourke's chord-melody toolkit.

quotes.verbatim_quote: You can see the diads, octaves, and passing chords being a feature in this rendition.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Catalogues the three load-bearing devices O'Rourke uses to drive an up-tempo arrangement: diads, octaves, and passing chords.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
I needed to manipulate the rhythm of the melody in places to facilitate easier jumps across the
fretboard.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the explicit rule that rhythmic adjustment of the melody is acceptable when fretboard jumps would otherwise be impractical.
